Peter begins this chapter with a seemingly simple command: rid yourselves of malice, deceit, hypocrisy, envy, and slander. But notice what follows immediately. He calls new believers to crave pure spiritual milk like newborn babies. The connection is stark and unsettling. You cannot grow in your salvation while holding onto these things. They are the poison that stunts spiritual growth. What makes this demand even sharper is that Peter anchors it in experience: you have tasted that the Lord is good. You know His goodness, and yet you are still tempted to harbor bitterness toward others, to deceive, to envy what they possess. Peter is not offering theoretical ethics here. He is confronting the gap between what you have experienced of God's character and how you actually live. The question becomes unavoidable: if you have tasted the Lord's goodness, why do you still taste malice?

What follows reveals something staggering about your identity if you believe in Christ. You are not merely an individual saved for personal comfort. You are a living stone being built into a spiritual house, part of a holy priesthood offering spiritual sacrifices to God through Jesus Christ. This is not decoration—it is your fundamental reality. Christ Himself is the cornerstone, rejected by the world but chosen and precious in God's sight. To those who reject Him, He is a stone of stumbling, an offense. But to you who believe, He is precious. And because you are united to Him, you have become a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for God's own possession. You exist for one purpose: to proclaim the virtues of Him who called you out of darkness into His marvelous light. This reorients everything. Your conduct matters not because you earn favor but because you display the character of the God you worship to a world that does not yet know Him.

The rest of the chapter applies this radical identity to your daily life, and here Peter's words grow uncomfortable. You are to submit to human institutions, to kings and governors, not because they deserve it but because submission itself glorifies God when done for His sake. Servants are to submit even to harsh masters. Why? Because suffering unjustly, when done consciously before God, is commendable. This is not resignation. It is active trust in a God who sees what others cannot see. Peter grounds this impossibly high calling in the suffering of Christ Himself. Jesus endured abuse without retaliation, made no threats, but entrusted Himself to Him who judges justly. He bore your sins in His body on the tree, not to make your life comfortable but to break the power of sin over you and call you to live in righteousness. You are like sheep who went astray, but you have now returned to the Shepherd and Overseer of your souls. He is watching. He is judging. He is faithful. This is the only foundation strong enough to support the life Peter is asking you to live—a life of honor, submission, and suffering that makes no sense except in light of a God who is just, good, and ultimately in control.
